notes: Official Monarch product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
As a self‑employed consultant with no dependents, the big three to protect are your income, your health, and your assets. Most people in your situation have a few gaps, so here’s a clear checklist to run through.

1. Health insurance & medical risk

One big medical event can wipe out savings, so start here.

You could review:

Do you have major medical coverage (ACA plan, private plan, or through a partner/association)?
What is your deductible and out‑of‑pocket maximum?
If you have a high‑deductible health plan (HDHP), you might consider:
Using a Health Savings Account (HSA) if eligible as a tax‑advantaged way to cover that big deductible.
If income is variable, many people:
Aim to keep at least one full deductible + a bit of buffer in savings.

Key idea: Know your worst‑case annual medical cost and have a plan (cash + HSA) to cover it.

2. Disability insurance (often the biggest gap for self‑employed)

Your future income is usually your largest asset, and it stops if you can’t work.

Gaps to check:

Do you have long‑term disability insurance at all? (Most self‑employed people don’t by default.)
Is it “own‑occupation” (pays if you can’t do your consulting work) or only if you can’t work any job?
Benefit level: many aim for around 60%+ of income coverage.
Waiting period: how long you must be disabled before benefits start (often 90 days).

If you find you have no coverage, this is often a top area self‑employed consultants evaluate.

3. Professional liability (Errors & Omissions)

If you give advice, design, or analysis, a client could claim your work caused them a loss.

You could check:

Do you have professional liability / E&O insurance?
Do your contracts:
Limit your liability (for example, to fees paid)?
Require you to carry certain coverage?
If you subcontract or use subcontractors, are you responsible for their errors?

Key idea: Make sure both your contracts and your insurance match the real risk in your work.

4. General business coverage

Your tools and operations are your livelihood.

You might review:

A Business owner’s policy (BOP) or rider that covers:
Your laptop, equipment, and data (especially if you travel or work from cafés/client sites).
General liability if clients visit your office or you work on their premises.
Cyber/data breach coverage if you handle sensitive client data.
If you work from home, note that homeowners/renters often don’t fully cover business equipment by default.
5. Personal liability & property

Even without dependents, you still have personal risk.

Check:

Renters or homeowners insurance:
Covers your stuff (fire, theft, etc.).
Includes personal liability if someone is injured at your home.
Auto insurance:
Liability limits (many people consider higher limits than state minimums).
Whether your policy allows business use of your car if you drive to clients.
If you have meaningful assets or high income, some people also look at:
Umbrella liability insurance (extra liability on top of auto/home).

Key idea: Make sure a single accident doesn’t put your personal finances on the line.

6. Life insurance (lower priority, but not always zero)

With no dependents, life insurance is often less critical, but not always irrelevant.

You might ask:

Do you have:
Co‑signed debts, a mortgage with someone else, or family who would be financially impacted?
A desire to cover final expenses so no one else has to?
If yes, some people choose a modest term life policy to cover those amounts.
7. Other areas to consider
Dental/vision: If large procedures would be a strain, some people prefer having coverage vs. self‑insuring.
Travel insurance: If you travel frequently for work, especially internationally.
Identity theft / cyber: If you store client data or are highly online.

Big picture:
For someone self‑employed with no dependents, the usual priority order is:

Health insurance + cash for your deductible
Disability insurance to protect your income
Professional + general business coverage
Personal liability (renters/home/auto, possibly umbrella)
Targeted life insurance only if someone else would be financially impacted

If you’d like, Monarch can go one layer deeper and:

Help you build a prioritized list (for example, top 2–3 to evaluate this month), and
Draft a question checklist you can use when talking to an insurance professional so you can compare options without getting pushed into something you don’t need.
Area	Why It Matters For You	Common Gaps To Check
Health insurance	One big medical event can wipe out savings	High deductible with no HSA savings, no out‑of‑pocket max awareness
Disability insurance	Your income stops if you can’t work	No long‑term disability, very low benefit amounts, exclusions for your occupation
Liability (professional)	Clients can sue over your work	No professional liability (E&O), unclear contract indemnity clauses
Liability (personal)	You can be sued outside of work	Low auto liability limits, no renters/homeowners, no umbrella
Business property & operations	Your tools and operations are your livelihood	No coverage for laptop/equipment, data loss, or business interruption
Life insurance	Less critical with no dependents, but still relevant in some cases	No coverage for final expenses or co‑signed debts (if applicable)
Other: dental/vision, identity, etc.	Quality of life and risk management	Relying only on cash for predictable but large expenses
